message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>This study aims to investigate coping strategies used by Bangladeshi citizens during the COVID-19 pandemic.</jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Prospective, cross-sectional survey of adults (N=2001) living in Bangladesh.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants were interviewed for socio-demographic data and completed the Bengali translated Brief-COPE Inventory. Statistical data analysis was conducted using SPSS (Version 20).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Participants (N=2001), aged 18 to 86 years, were recruited from eight administrative divisions within Bangladesh (mean age 31.85±14.2 years). Male to female participant ratio was 53.4% (n=1074) to 46.6% (n=927). Higher scores were reported for
                    <jats:italic>approach</jats:italic>
                    coping styles (29.83±8.9), with lower scores reported for
                    <jats:italic>avoidant</jats:italic>
                    coping styles (20.83 ± 6.05).
                    <jats:italic>Humor</jats:italic>
                    coping scores were reported at 2.68±1.3 and
                    <jats:italic>religion</jats:italic>
                    coping scores at 5.64±1.8. Both men and women showed similar coping styles. Multivariate analysis found a significant relationship between male gender and both
                    <jats:italic>humor</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>avoidant</jats:italic>
                    coping (p <.01). Male gender was found to be inversely related to both
                    <jats:italic>religion</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>approach</jats:italic>
                    coping (p <.01). Marital status and education were significantly related to all coping style domains (p<.01). Occupation was significantly related to
                    <jats:italic>approach</jats:italic>
                    coping (p <.01). Rural and urban locations differed significantly in participant coping styles (p <.01). Factor analysis revealed two cluster groups (Factor 1 and 2) comprised of unique combinations from all coping style domains.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Participants in this study coped with the COVID-19 pandemic by utilizing a combination of coping strategies. Factor 1 revealed both
                    <jats:italic>avoidant</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>approach</jats:italic>
                    coping strategies and Factor 2 revealed a combination of
                    <jats:italic>humor</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>avoidant</jats:italic>
                    coping strategies. Overall, a higher utilization of
                    <jats:italic>approach</jats:italic>
                    coping strategies was reported, which has previously been associated with better physical and mental health outcomes.
                    <jats:italic>Religion</jats:italic>
                    was found to be a coping strategy for all participants. Future research may focus on understanding resilience in vulnerable populations, including people with disability or with migrant or refugee status in Bangladesh.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Coping with COVID-19 Pandemic: A Population-Based Study in Bangladesh
